metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the title and body: either rename the test to 'intra-process lock-identity test' and narrow the 'Why' claim accordingly, OR add a subprocess-based variant (e.g., using `multiprocessing` or `subprocess`) that actually exercises the cross-process canonicalization guarantee.
findings.finding: The title promises a 'cross-process' lock-identity test, but the body explicitly delivers intra-process testing: 'separate open() descriptions to the same file contend even within one process.' fcntl/flock semantics differ between intra-process and cross-process contexts on Linux (within one process, flock locks are per open-file-description; across processes the exclusion is also per open-file-description but the OS-level guarantee is the one callers of the production API actually depend on). The test as described does not prove the cross-process invariant the title and 'Why' section promise.
findings.location: Title / 'What' section — 'separate open() descriptions to the same file contend even within one process'
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (minimal): Rename to 'Add canonical_tracker symlink/realpath lock-identity test' and update the Why to clarify the intra-process guarantee being tested. Option B (complete): Add a second test that spawns a child process holding the lock via one path while the parent tries to acquire via the other path, proving the cross-process invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number citation: change `acquire canonicalizes at lock.py:289` to `lock.py:282` (the actual `tracker = canonical_tracker(tracker)` call).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'acquire canonicalizes at lock.py:289' but the actual canonicalization call is at line 282 (`tracker = canonical_tracker(tracker)`); line 289 is the `LockTimeout` raise on fcntl failure. The cited line number is factually wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: In the '## Why' section, change: '`acquire` canonicalizes at `lock.py:289`' → '`acquire` canonicalizes at `lock.py:282`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'What' section describing the post-release re-acquisition step (i.e., after releasing the first holder, the second acquirer via the alternate path must successfully complete `acquire()`) so AC1 and AC2 are fully grounded in the implementation description.
findings.finding: The 'after release, the symlink caller acquires' success step appears in AC1 and AC2 but is not described anywhere in the What section, leaving that verification step without a corresponding implementation description.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 and AC2 vs. the 'What' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, after the LockTimeout paragraph, add: 'Each test also verifies the positive case: once the first holder releases the lock, the second acquirer (via the alternate path) completes `acquire()` successfully, confirming the canonicalization does not permanently bind the lock to one path representation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the symlink and tracker directory are created inside a `tmp_path`-scoped (or equivalent `tempfile.mkdtemp` + teardown) fixture so the test lifecycle (CREATE → USE → RETIRE) is fully defined and isolated.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the symlink or temporary directory is created and cleaned up in the test. The symlink is test-local shared state whose CREATE and RETIRE lifecycle is unspecified; if the test uses a hardcoded path or a module-scoped fixture without teardown, the symlink could persist and cause false positives or interference in subsequent test runs.
findings.location: Scope / 'What' section — no fixture or temp-dir strategy mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fixture line such as: `symlink_path = tmp_path / 'tracker_link'; symlink_path.symlink_to(real_tracker_path)` inside the test body or a function-scoped fixture, relying on pytest's `tmp_path` for automatic cleanup.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
